quick_facts.label: Role focus
quick_facts.value: Plans, coordinates and monitors CSR programmes, typically working with implementing NGOs, community representatives and internal corporate teams.

quick_facts.label: Indian regulatory context
quick_facts.value: For companies meeting the statutory thresholds, CSR work is governed by section 135 of the Companies Act, 2013 and the Companies (CSR Policy) Rules, 2014; MCA’s FAQ lists thresholds of net worth of ₹500 crore, turnover of ₹1,000 crore, or net profit of ₹5 crore in the immediately preceding financial year.

quick_facts.label: Education route
quick_facts.value: An MSW is a relevant preparation route: university MSW curricula include CSR, community organisation, social-work research, practicum/fieldwork and internships, while CSR employers may prefer a master’s degree in social work.

quick_facts.label: Work setting
quick_facts.value: The role can combine office-based project, budget and reporting work with travel to project locations for field monitoring and engagement with beneficiaries and NGO partners.

sections.heading: What a Corporate Social Responsibility Executive does
paragraphs: A CSR executive supports the full programme cycle: translating the organisation’s CSR priorities into project plans, coordinating execution with NGOs or other implementing partners, and tracking budgets, timelines, outputs and outcomes.

In Indian companies subject to the CSR framework, the work also has a governance dimension. The CSR Committee’s annual action plan can cover approved projects, execution arrangements, fund-use and implementation schedules, monitoring and reporting mechanisms, and need or impact assessments. Executives commonly supply the documentation, field evidence, partner coordination and management information needed for these processes.

Field monitoring is a material part of many roles: it can include visiting project sites, reviewing partner records, engaging community groups, identifying delivery issues and escalating corrective action.
bullets: Develop project plans, budgets, indicators and implementation schedules.
Coordinate NGO partners, community stakeholders, finance, procurement, communications and corporate CSR teams.
Review progress reports and expenditure/utilisation information; maintain project documentation and periodic MIS.
Conduct or support site visits, partner reviews, beneficiary tracking and outcome/impact measurement.
Support statutory, internal and annual CSR reporting and communications.

sections.heading: Entry routes from an MSW
paragraphs: An MSW is a strong route into CSR because programmes commonly combine social-work theory with community organisation, research, welfare administration, practicum and internship/fieldwork. Some MSW curricula also offer dedicated CSR coursework that covers legal provisions, corporate–community collaboration, business ethics and CSR strategy.

For a candidate targeting this role, field placement or early experience with an NGO, community-development project, foundation, monitoring and evaluation assignment, or corporate social-impact programme can make the MSW learning directly demonstrable. Employers’ role descriptions often seek an ability to manage projects and partners, assess implementation, report results and understand CSR compliance.

MSW is relevant but is not the only pathway: current postings can also accept related social-science, development, management or finance backgrounds. Requirements vary by employer, responsibility level and sector.
bullets: Complete an MSW with fieldwork, research and, where available, CSR/community-development electives.
Build a portfolio of project reports, needs assessments, monitoring tools, budgets, presentations or impact documentation produced during placement or internship.
Apply to entry or early-career titles such as CSR Executive, CSR Project Coordinator, Programme Associate, Foundation Associate or Monitoring and Evaluation Associate.
Develop practical familiarity with NGO due diligence, grant/project documentation, outcome indicators and reporting requirements.

sections.heading: Skills to build
paragraphs: The core capability is structured social-project management: planning activities and budgets, coordinating delivery, recording evidence, reviewing partner reports and reporting progress against agreed milestones, outputs and outcomes.

Relationship management is equally important. CSR executives work across NGO partners, communities and internal functions, so they need clear written and verbal communication, meeting facilitation, respectful community engagement and the judgement to flag implementation risks or deviations.

Analytical and compliance skills matter in India’s regulated CSR environment. Useful capabilities include Excel-based tracking, presentation and report writing, interpreting budgets and expenditure data, setting indicators, and understanding the company’s CSR policy and applicable statutory requirements.
bullets: Project planning, scheduling, budgeting and partner coordination.
Monitoring, evaluation and learning: indicators, field verification, beneficiary/outcome tracking and basic data analysis.
Professional documentation: proposals, MoUs or project records, utilisation/progress reports, MIS and presentations.
Stakeholder communication with NGOs, local communities, employees and internal management.
Working knowledge of CSR compliance, reporting and financial-accountability processes; advanced spreadsheet and presentation skills.

sections.heading: Where and how the work is done
paragraphs: CSR executives may work in a company’s CSR or sustainability team, a corporate foundation, a professional-services or implementation organisation, or an NGO that manages CSR-funded projects. The day-to-day mix varies: corporate roles may emphasise governance, budget control and reporting, while implementation-facing roles may have more frequent community and project-site engagement.

The work is usually hybrid in function rather than necessarily in location: desk work covers planning, approvals, data consolidation and reports; field visits are used to verify implementation quality, discuss challenges with partners and communities, and review evidence of results.

Programme themes are employer-specific. Current role descriptions include areas such as water and soil, sanitation, sustainable agriculture, livelihoods, health and nutrition, education, inclusion and environmental initiatives.
bullets: Corporate office or foundation: strategy support, governance, grant/project oversight and reporting.
Project sites: monitoring, community meetings, partner reviews and implementation verification.
Cross-functional coordination: finance, procurement, legal, communications, HR and employee-volunteering teams may all be involved.

sections.heading: Possible career progression
paragraphs: With demonstrated capability in managing several projects, partners, budgets and reporting cycles, an executive can progress to CSR associate, programme manager, CSR manager or foundation-management roles. More senior roles increasingly involve portfolio strategy, NGO due diligence and onboarding, grant governance, impact-assessment commissioning, risk management and leadership of cross-functional teams.

A second path is specialisation: monitoring and evaluation, social-impact measurement, thematic programme design (for example livelihoods, health, education or climate), ESG/social data, partnerships, or corporate-foundation operations. The appropriate next role depends on the candidate’s field experience, analytical depth and exposure to regulatory and financial processes.
bullets: Build breadth by managing projects from proposal and partner selection through closure.
Build depth through a specialism in monitoring and evaluation, impact assessment, grants, ESG/social reporting or a development theme.
Seek responsibility for budgets, compliance records, partner due diligence, senior stakeholder updates and project portfolios.
